headline: Seven Tankers, One Phone Call: Malaysia Off Iran's Safe List, Cleared by PM Direct Appeal
context: Seven Malaysian-company tankers were awaiting Hormuz clearance by 28 March 2026. Malaysia was not on Iran's original safe-passage list. PM Anwar secured approval by direct call; by mid-April only two vessels had transited and one had technical problems.

cards.big: Iran approved all seven Malaysian-company tankers after PM Anwar called Iran's president. Transit happened in stages.
cards.sub: Three Petronas, two MISC, one Sapura, one Sepadu vessel. Iran's original blanket safe list was China, Russia, India, Iraq, Pakistan. Malaysia was not on it.

cards.big: Malaysia is a CMF member on paper, but no RMN naval escort was required or deployed for these vessels.
cards.sub: South Korea's permanent Cheonghae Unit operates in the Gulf of Aden. Japan's SDF mission is information-gathering in the Middle East; Japan's MOD says its area excludes the Strait of Hormuz.

cards.big: Brent crude hit $126/barrel. The subsidy bill jumped to RM4 billion per month.
cards.sub: Higher crude can raise Petronas revenue and add to fuel subsidy outlay simultaneously, depending on hedging and assumptions.

cards.big: Putrajaya was reported to stop accepting port-of-call applications from US warships during the episode.
cards.sub: Anwar said Malaysia takes a balanced and independent stance. ~20,000 seafarers were reported trapped in the Persian Gulf at one point. Industry reporting cited a 90% traffic drop.

cards.big: The question is not whether the ships got through. It is whether a trade-dependent nation can keep relying on personal calls when the next strait closes.
Diplomacy worked here. CMF membership without operational presence, plus reliance on a direct PM call, are the structural features the episode exposed.
